metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or a gate condition in AC2) that verifies `docs/your-first-change.md` exists in the repo before or at the time lockdown.yml merges — e.g. `test -f docs/your-first-change.md` as part of the workflow's CI or as an explicit depends_on enforcement step.
findings.finding: The plan describes `docs/your-first-change.md` as a hard precondition ('exists before this lands — this story depends_on the onboarding story'), but no Acceptance Criterion verifies that the file actually exists at that path. AC2 only checks that the link string appears in the workflow file (grep of lockdown.yml), which passes even if the onboarding story never landed and the linked document is absent — leaving every auto-closed PR pointing to a broken link.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC2 with: 'File existence check — prove: `test -f docs/your-first-change.md` passes in CI at merge time; this story must not merge before the onboarding story that creates that file.' Alternatively, promote the depends_on relationship to a formal blocking dependency in the Scope section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that the onboarding story creating `docs/your-first-change.md` is listed as a hard `depends_on` blocker (not just a note), OR add a fallback link (e.g., CONTRIBUTING.md) so AC2 can be satisfied if the dependency hasn't landed yet.
findings.finding: `docs/your-first-change.md` is referenced as a pre-existing file ('exists before this lands — this story depends_on the onboarding story') and is deep-linked in the bot comment message (AC2 content check), but the file does not exist anywhere in the repository.
findings.location: What section (bot comment message) and AC2: 'a link to `docs/your-first-change.md`'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify the exact GitHub API field name and availability for disabling pull requests on a repository (the plan cites `has_pull_requests: false` as a 'Feb-2026 native setting') before writing E1's procedure, and update both the plan's Why section and E1's description to name the confirmed field or mechanism.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'GitHub's native Feb-2026 `has_pull_requests: false` setting' as an established capability in the Why section and as the basis for E1's 'rejected alternative' documentation in ADR 0037 — but this named API field is unverified and a prior review of this plan (cached at `.rebar/cache/plan-review/fc2a-9fa5-def6-4e68/eb635a03c3cd1417ceca.json`) specifically flagged that this field may not exist as named. If the field name or its UX behaviour is different from what the plan assumes, E1's scope is mis-specified and ADR 0037 will record an inaccurate rationale for the rejected alternative.
findings.location: Why section: 'Chromium/Qt/torvalds flipped GitHub's native Feb-2026 `has_pull_requests: false` setting'; Pre-flight experiment E1; ADR 0037 scope (native-disable rejection)
findings.suggested_fix: Retitle E1 as 'Scratch-repo test of GitHub's native PR-disable UX (confirm the API field name at execution — the UI setting is in repo Settings → General → "Pull Requests"; the REST API PATCH field is `allow_forking`-adjacent; verify via `gh api repos/{owner}/{repo} --jq keys` which fields are writable)'. Update the Why section to say 'the native UI/API disable option' without asserting the specific field name until E1 confirms it.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the `has_pull_requests: false` field reference in E1 and the Why section with the actual GitHub mechanism for suppressing PR merge UI (removing all merge methods via `allow_merge_commit=false` + `allow_squash_merge=false` + `allow_rebase_merge=false`, combined with the existing merge-blocking ruleset), and reframe E1 as testing that UX rather than a non-existent boolean toggle.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that GitHub has a native `has_pull_requests: false` repository setting (introduced Feb 2026, described as used by Chromium/Qt/torvalds), but this field does not exist in the GitHub REST API PATCH /repos endpoint. The repo's own runbook (`infra/runbooks/github-mirror-lock.md`, lines 200–202) explicitly states: 'Disable Pull Requests is not a single repo flag.' E1 as written ('Scratch-repo test of `has_pull_requests: false` fork-side UX') is un-executable, and the ADR's 'rejected alternatives' section will contain a factually incorrect description of the native-disable mechanism.
findings.location: Why section ('native Feb-2026 `has_pull_requests: false` setting'); Pre-flight experiments E1; AC5 ('E1–E4 outcomes recorded'); ADR 0037 scope
findings.suggested_fix: In E1, replace 'Scratch-repo test of `has_pull_requests: false` fork-side UX' with 'Scratch-repo test of native PR-suppression UX: disable all merge methods (`allow_merge_commit=false`, `allow_squash_merge=false`, `allow_rebase_merge=false` via `gh api -X PATCH`) and observe the contributor-facing UI — documents the rejected alternative in the ADR.' Update the Why section to reflect that the 'native disable' peers used merge-method removal rather than a single boolean field.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Tighten AC4 to require a live GitHub Actions workflow run with a real `dependabot[bot]`-actor event (e.g., an actual Dependabot-opened PR, or a repository_dispatch that provably sets `github.actor` to `dependabot[bot]` in the workflow log) — remove 'simulated actor test' as a standalone sufficient alternative.
findings.finding: AC4 explicitly endorses 'a simulated actor test' as a sufficient alternative to 'a Dependabot-authored PR' for proving the exemption. If the simulation is implemented as anything other than a real GitHub Actions workflow run triggered by an actual `dependabot[bot]`-actor event, the job-level `if: github.actor != 'dependabot[bot]'` conditional is never exercised live, leaving the defaulted exemption path unvalidated end-to-end.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the AC4 parenthetical with: 'prove by the workflow run log of an actual Dependabot PR (or a workflow_dispatch test that sets actor via a real bot token) showing the job was skipped due to the `if:` condition.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] AC4: Replace 'or a simulated actor test' with a concrete verification method — e.g., open a maintainer-authored PR from the repo's own bot account with `actor` set, OR document that verification is done by observing the FIRST real Dependabot advisory PR after go-live and recording its outcome in the ticket; remove the unspecified 'simulated actor test' phrasing.
findings.finding: AC4's Dependabot exemption proof defers to 'a simulated actor test' without specifying the simulation mechanism, making it both a placeholder decision (E2) and an unverifiable end-to-end check (E6).
findings.suggested_fix: State a concrete fallback: 'If no Dependabot PR is naturally triggered within 30 days of landing, the maintainer will manually invoke `act pull_request_target -e event.json` with actor=dependabot[bot] using nektos/act and record the output as proof.' Alternatively, add a second job that echoes actor and assert the condition statically via `yq` on the workflow file: `yq '.jobs.bot.if' .github/workflows/lockdown.yml | grep -q "dependabot"`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] AC1: Add a deterministic CLI proof command, e.g. `gh pr view <PR_NUMBER> --json state,comments --jq '[.state == "CLOSED", (.comments[0].body | test("read-only mirror"))] | all'`, replacing the 'timeline shows' visual instruction.
findings.finding: AC1's proof method ('the test PR's timeline shows the bot comment + closed state') is a manual visual inspection, not a deterministic command — it cannot be automated or audited reproducibly.
findings.suggested_fix: gh pr view <PR_NUMBER> --json state,comments --jq '[.state == "CLOSED", (.comments | map(.body) | any(test("read-only mirror")))] | all'
Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `lockdown.yml` declares `permissions: { pull-requests: write }` at the **workflow level** (top of file, not only job level), so any future jobs added to the file inherit a deny-all default unless they explicitly override — matching the repo's existing convention in `gerrit-verify.yaml` and GitHub's `pull_request_target` hardening guidance.
findings.finding: The plan specifies `permissions: { pull-requests: write }` but does not explicitly state this must be declared at the WORKFLOW level (not only at the job level). GitHub Actions evaluates token permissions hierarchically: if the workflow lacks a top-level `permissions:` block, the repo's default token permission setting applies to any jobs that don't individually declare permissions. For a `pull_request_target` workflow that may grow over time, the absence of a workflow-level deny-all default (`permissions: {}` at the top, with only the needed grant at the job level) leaves the workflow one inadvertent new-job addition away from running with overly broad token rights against untrusted fork input.
findings.location: What section — `.github/workflows/lockdown.yml` spec, 'Explicit least-privilege block: permissions: { pull-requests: write }'
findings.suggested_fix: In the lockdown.yml spec, add: 'The `permissions:` block is placed at the workflow level (top of the file, outside any `jobs:` key) so the default for all jobs is `pull-requests: write` only; no job-level override is needed unless a future job requires a different scope.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify `docs/your-first-change.md` exists before merging this story (or add a hard `depends_on` gate, a placeholder file, or an interim fallback URL in the bot message); AC2's link proof is vacuous if the file is absent.
findings.finding: `docs/your-first-change.md` does not exist in the repository — the plan asserts it 'exists before this lands' and excludes it from this story's scope, but the file is absent from `docs/`, making the bot-message deep-link and the AC2 grep proof impossible to satisfy until the dependency lands.
findings.location: What section — comment message bullet: 'deep-link Gerrit … + `docs/your-first-change.md` (exists before this lands — this story depends_on the onboarding story)'; AC line 2: 'a link to `docs/your-first-change.md`'
findings.suggested_fix: Add an explicit blocking dependency marker (e.g., `blocked_by: <onboarding-story-id>`) and specify a fallback: if the file doesn't exist at merge time, the bot message should link to CONTRIBUTING.md instead, updated to the final URL once the onboarding story lands.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `infra/runbooks/github-mirror-lock.md` to the edit set and update its `--description` argument to match the new repo description string.
findings.finding: `infra/runbooks/github-mirror-lock.md` already contains a repo description string (line 214: 'Read-only mirror of Gerrit (rebar.solutions.navateam.com). Contribute via Gerrit — see CONTRIBUTING.md.') that differs from the description this plan will set ('Read-only mirror — code review at https://rebar.solutions.navateam.com'), but the runbook is not enumerated as a file to update for consistency.
findings.location: Scope / What section: 'Update the GitHub repo description'; AC7: 'Repo description updated'
findings.suggested_fix: In the What section, add: 'Update `infra/runbooks/github-mirror-lock.md` line ~214 to reflect the new description string.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] AC2: Supplement the workflow-file grep with a `gh pr view <PR_NUMBER> --json comments --jq` assertion that checks the ACTUAL posted comment body for both required strings and forbidden strings, anchoring the proof to the rendered output, not the source template.
findings.finding: The negative-string grep in AC2 ('contains none of "rejected", "not accepted", "closed as invalid"') is unanchored and checks only the workflow file text, not the rendered comment — if the action wraps the message in a variable or reads from a file, a passing grep would not confirm the rendered output is free of those strings.
findings.suggested_fix: gh pr view <PR_NUMBER> --json comments --jq '.comments[0].body | [test("read-only mirror"), test("nothing is wrong with your change"), (test("rejected|not accepted|closed as invalid") | not)] | all'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Amend AC2 to verify message content by inspecting the ACTUAL POSTED COMMENT on the live test PR (e.g., via `gh pr view <pr-number> --comments | grep 'read-only mirror'`), not solely by grepping the workflow source file — move the source grep to a supplementary structural check only.
findings.finding: AC2 gates all message-content verification on grepping the WORKFLOW SOURCE FILE rather than inspecting the actual comment posted on the live test PR timeline. This is a self-authored-oracle / source-grep-masquerading-as-behavioral-test pattern: the grep proves the strings exist in the template, not that the bot actually posts them. If the action's `comment` input key is renamed in v5, the template is loaded from an external file, or the action silently truncates the message, the grep passes while the real posted comment is wrong or absent.
findings.suggested_fix: In AC1, after confirming the PR is closed, add: `gh pr view <test-pr-number> --comments --jq '.[0].body'` (or equivalent) and assert it contains each required string and none of the prohibited strings. Retain the source grep as a secondary sanity check but do not let it substitute for runtime output verification.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Record the observed `dessant/repo-lockdown` v5 input key names (looked up from the action's published action.yml, not inferred from memory) directly in the plan's lockdown.yml bullet before implementation begins, so AC3's 'verified against pinned action.yml' check has a baseline to compare against.
findings.finding: The plan defers verification of `dessant/repo-lockdown` v5 input key names to execution time, explicitly noting 'a renamed input fails silently.' While this is acknowledged risk, the plan does not record the currently-known input surface (e.g., whether `close-pr` and `lock-pr` are confirmed v5 key names), leaving the workflow's correctness unverifiable from the plan itself. An unverified input name on a `pull_request_target` workflow that runs on every fork PR open is a silent runtime failure with no error signal.
findings.location: What section — lockdown.yml bullet: '`close-pr: true`, `lock-pr: false` (LibreOffice pattern) … Verify the v5 input key names against the action's manifest (action.yml) at execution; a renamed input fails silently.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
